annotation_items.explanation: Use 'in case' + clause for anticipated problems; 'for any occasion could happen' is ungrammatical word order.
'Share' requires the person introduced with 'with' (share X with Y).

annotation_items.text: I just mind blown from my disk C
annotation_items.correction: I'm just mind-blown by my disk C
annotation_items.explanation: Missing auxiliary verb and wrong preposition: say 'I'm/ I was mind-blown by ...'.

annotation_items.text: fed up from Codex and Claude
annotation_items.correction: fed up with Codex and Claude
annotation_items.explanation: The correct preposition with 'fed up' is 'with', not 'from'.

annotation_items.text: It eats free space every time
annotation_items.correction: It keeps using up free space
annotation_items.explanation: 'Eat (free) space' is an unusual collocation here; use 'use up' or 'take up' space for this meaning.
Present simple requires do-support: 'I don't understand' (or 'I'm not understanding' in limited contexts).

annotation_items.correction: what I should have done correctly
annotation_items.explanation: After 'should' use the perfect infinitive 'have done' to talk about past actions.
Use the plural demonstrative 'these' with a plural noun ('apps').
Incorrect article and adjective phrasing; use 'an unresolved issue' rather than 'the not resolved issue'.

annotation_items.text: they downloaded updates and keep it on disk
annotation_items.correction: they download updates and keep them on disk
annotation_items.explanation: For a habitual/general action use present simple ('download') and match the plural pronoun ('them').
Refers to 'updates' (plural) so the pronoun should be 'them', not 'it'.

annotation_items.text: So currently try to fix it
annotation_items.correction: So I'm currently trying to fix it
annotation_items.explanation: Missing subject/auxiliary and progressive form: use 'I'm ... trying' to describe an ongoing action.

annotation_items.correction: exercises for us to practice speaking
annotation_items.explanation: Unnatural preposition sequence; use 'for us to practice speaking' to express purpose.
A countable noun ('question') needs an article ('a' or 'the') in this context.

annotation_items.text: Day was a really big day
annotation_items.correction: Today was a really big day
annotation_items.explanation: Spoken omission: the noun 'Day' needs a determiner or replacement ('Today' or 'It was') to be grammatical.
Missing definite article before a specific framework: use 'the' with 'Star Framework way' or rephrase 'using the STAR framework'.
Subject pronoun case and conventional order: use 'my brother and I' as the subject, not 'me'.
Count noun missing an article. Before a singular countable noun use 'a/an'.
After 'The main idea is' use an infinitive ('to prepare') rather than a present-participial clause here.
Omit 'of' or add 'the' for 'most': 'most designers' or 'most of the designers'.
Wrong preposition/structure: use an infinitive ('to help') or 'to help them' rather than 'for help' here.
Missing indefinite article before a singular countable noun: 'an early prototype'.
Missing definite article before a specific noun phrase: 'the quality' of something.
Wrong preposition for testing participants: use 'with' (tested with X people), not 'on'.
Use 'in' or 'at' for events/meetings; 'on this meeting' is incorrect.
Omit 'of' or add 'the' for 'most': 'most people' is correct.
Wrong tense: narrative refers to past events, so use past simple 'built'.
Wrong preposition and missing article: say 'on the internet'.

annotation_items.text: before we already built AI Guide
annotation_items.correction: before we had already built the AI Guide
annotation_items.explanation: For an action that happened earlier than another past action, use past perfect ('had already built'); also 'the' before a named product is likely.
Countable noun phrase needs an indefinite article: 'a warm audience'.
Use 'on' with 'channel' (a platform), not 'in'.
Missing definite article before a specific product: 'the product' or pluralize to 'products'.

annotation_items.text: I'm just not understand it clearly
annotation_items.correction: I just don't understand it clearly
annotation_items.explanation: The verb phrase is incorrect: after 'I'm just not' you need the auxiliary + base verb ('don't understand') or progressive ('am not understanding').

annotation_items.text: just can do tasks on their work
annotation_items.explanation: The preposition 'on' is incorrect here; use 'at work'. Word order simplified for natural speech.

annotation_items.text: we a bit different from them
annotation_items.correction: we are a bit different from them
annotation_items.explanation: The verb 'are' (copula) is missing after the subject 'we'.

annotation_items.correction: make a career in any company
annotation_items.explanation: English requires the article 'a' with the count noun 'career'.

annotation_items.text: and we built something that people want to buy
annotation_items.correction: and we've built something that people want to buy
annotation_items.explanation: With present relevance ('we can tell the story') the present perfect ('we've built') is more appropriate than simple past; otherwise use consistent past time reference.

annotation_items.text: which one from these two parts
annotation_items.correction: which one of these two parts
annotation_items.explanation: Use 'one of' to select an item from a set; 'from' is incorrect collocation here.

annotation_items.text: realized that you can make a product
annotation_items.correction: realized that you could make a product
annotation_items.explanation: After a past reporting verb ('realized') English usually backshifts modals ('can' → 'could').

annotation_items.text: you sold your product people who are not previously interested in it
annotation_items.correction: you sold your product to people who were not previously interested in it
annotation_items.explanation: The verb 'sold' needs the preposition 'to' for the indirect object; also past reference ('were') fits 'previously'.
'Auditory' is the wrong word here; English speakers say 'audience' or 'customers' to mean a receptive group.
Missing definite article 'the' before 'question' and the adverb 'correctly' is required to modify 'understood'.
The preposition 'against' is incorrect in this context; 'for' indicates the intended users or beneficiaries.
Missing noun; 'office' alone doesn't specify the role (use 'office workers').
Count noun should be plural to match context ('NPCs').
Missing indefinite article 'a' before 'name'.

annotation_items.text: that you created it and you sold it
annotation_items.explanation: Redundant object pronoun 'it' combined with 'that' — omit repeated 'it'.
'Regarding me' is non-idiomatic in this context; 'as for me' is the usual conversational phrase.
Extra 'it's' makes the phrase ungrammatical; the fixed idiom is 'the sky is the limit.'
'Such kind of' is redundant; use 'such moments' or 'this kind of moment.'

annotation_items.text: for me it like a platform
annotation_items.correction: for me it's like a platform
annotation_items.explanation: Missing verb 'is' after subject 'it'.

annotation_items.text: from which one you can skyrocket on
annotation_items.explanation: Unnecessary word 'one' and redundant preposition 'on' — correct structure is 'from which you can skyrocket.'
Missing definite article 'the' before 'moment'.

annotation_items.correction: in my way of doing it / my way
annotation_items.explanation: 'In my way' is an unnatural collocation here; use 'my way' or 'in my way of doing it.'
Missing definite article 'the' with the time expression 'in the past'.

annotation_items.correction: with a designer who wants to
annotation_items.explanation: Missing indefinite article before singular countable noun 'designer'.
Subject-verb agreement: singular 'who' (referring to 'designer') requires 'wants'.
Missing indefinite article before the singular countable noun 'usability test'.

annotation_items.text: and to just try to sell
annotation_items.explanation: Unnecessary infinitive marker 'to' after 'and' here — use the bare verb 'try'.
Missing preposition 'of' in the phrase 'a bit of ...'.

annotation_items.correction: it's an important part of the product
annotation_items.explanation: Missing article(s): needs 'an' before 'important' and 'the' before 'product' for a specific noun.
Unnatural collocation; native speakers say 'the other side' rather than 'the opposite side'.
Standard collocation is 'interesting to me' rather than 'interesting for me'.
Incorrect word order and missing auxiliary for a question in English; use 'do' + subject + verb.
Missing indefinite article before the singular countable noun 'domain'.
Missing indefinite article before the singular countable noun 'digital product'.
Use 'for' (scheme for selling) rather than 'of' in this context; 'scheme of selling' is not a natural preposition choice.
The noun 'part' requires a determiner here: 'the upselling part' or 'an upselling part'.

annotation_items.correction: be more involved with your product
annotation_items.explanation: 'Be deeper in your product' is an unnatural collocation; use 'be more involved with/ in your product' or 'be more familiar with your product'.
'My part' is unnatural here; say 'my responsibility'/'my role' to express that something is your duty.

annotation_items.text: understand your clients better or clearly
annotation_items.correction: understand your clients better or more clearly
annotation_items.explanation: The adverb 'clearly' is awkward here; use 'more clearly' to compare levels of understanding, and keep it after 'understand'.
A singular countable noun requires an article: 'an absurd position'.

annotation_items.text: where you shouldn't be agree with it.
annotation_items.correction: where you shouldn't agree with it.
annotation_items.explanation: After modal 'shouldn't' use the bare infinitive ('agree'), not 'be' + past participle.
Two modals cannot combine ('can shouldn't'); use the single correct modal 'shouldn't' to express prohibition or advice against doing something.

annotation_items.correction: I suggest taking / I suggest we take
annotation_items.explanation: After 'suggest' use the gerund ('suggest taking') or a clause ('suggest we take'), not the infinitive 'to take'.

annotation_items.text: would you like to defend where you agree or not?
annotation_items.correction: whether you'd like to defend it or not / whether you agree or disagree
annotation_items.explanation: The structure is incorrect: use 'whether' to present two alternatives ('whether you'd like to defend it or not') or rephrase to 'defend it or argue against it.'
Standard English uses 'I don't agree' (or 'I'm not agreeing') rather than 'I'm not agree'.
Question should use auxiliary 'do' in present simple: 'You don't agree?'

annotation_items.correction: start from my point of view
annotation_items.explanation: 'start from my position' is a literal phrasing; native speakers say 'start from my point of view' or 'from my perspective'.
Missing definite article 'the' before 'other side'.
Subject-verb agreement: plural 'models' requires verb 'look' (not 'looks').
'cooperator' is not a natural collocation in this context; use 'collaborator' or 'partner'.

annotation_items.text: it even more smart than you
annotation_items.correction: they are even smarter than you
annotation_items.explanation: Pronoun and verb form mismatch and comparative form: plural subject needs 'they are' and 'smarter' (not 'more smart').

annotation_items.correction: collaborate with AI / work with AI
annotation_items.explanation: Unnatural collocation: native speakers usually say 'collaborate with' or 'work with' rather than 'match with' in this context.
Unnatural verb choice: 'force your ideas' is incorrect here; likely intended 'reinforce' or 'strengthen'.

annotation_items.text: ask them something you even know
annotation_items.correction: ask them about something you already know
annotation_items.explanation: Missing preposition 'about' and awkward adverb placement; say 'ask them about something you already know.'

annotation_items.correction: be a newcomer much more frequently
annotation_items.explanation: Missing indefinite article 'a' before 'newcomer'.
English needs the auxiliary 'do' for a negative with the verb 'agree' in this simple-present statement; 'I'm not agree' is ungrammatical.
Use the comparative adjective form and the common repetition pattern 'lazier and lazier' instead of repeating 'lazy'.

annotation_items.text: last year you do your common routine
annotation_items.correction: last year you did your routine
annotation_items.explanation: Reference to 'last year' requires past tense ('did'), not present ('do').

annotation_items.text: for you it's not necessary to think
annotation_items.correction: it's not necessary for you to think
annotation_items.explanation: Word order: the phrase 'necessary for you' is the correct pattern (or 'you don't need to think').
A specific noun ('solution') here needs a determiner ('the') or pluralization; 'delegate solution' is missing the article.
Singular, specific noun phrase needs the definite article 'the'.

annotation_items.correction: create a new way of interaction
annotation_items.explanation: 'Way' is a countable noun and requires an article ('a new way').

annotation_items.text: Every time new project needs new references
annotation_items.correction: Every new project needs new references
annotation_items.explanation: Either add an article ('a new project') or rephrase as 'Every new project...'; the original lacks the necessary determiner.
Either pluralize 'solution' ('solutions') or add an article ('a generic design solution'); the singular without article is incorrect here.
The usual prepositions with 'internet' are 'on' or 'across' — 'in the whole internet' is not idiomatic.

annotation_items.text: Before, designers want to build something great for themselves
annotation_items.correction: Before, designers wanted to build something great for themselves
annotation_items.explanation: The time adverb 'Before' refers to the past, so the verb should be past tense ('wanted').
There is an extra, redundant 'to' in the infinitive phrase; also adjust the reflexive pronoun to agree with 'designers'.
With the indefinite singular 'someone' the verb needs an -s ('uses') or rephrase as 'some people use'.
'soft' is a non-standard short form; the correct noun is 'software'.
When using 'most' with a plural noun, omit 'of' unless followed by a determiner ('most of the designers').
Subject-verb agreement error: plural subject 'pages' requires plural verb 'look', not 'looks'.

annotation_items.text: I think not too close and not too far
annotation_items.correction: I think it's not too close and not too far
annotation_items.explanation: Missing copula ('it's'). The speaker needs a linking verb to form a full clause: 'I think it's not...'.
Redundant article/determiner: 'an' + 'another one' is ungrammatical; use 'another portfolio' or 'one more portfolio'.
Missing possessive and gerund: English prefers a possessive + gerund ('Claude's ways of designing' or 'the Claude way of designing').
The verb 'reply' takes the preposition 'to' before an indirect object ('replied to me').
Redundant conjunctions 'and but' are ungrammatical; use a single connector ('but') here.
The usual preposition is 'in' (or 'during') for events like 'an interview' ('in your interview' or 'during your interview').
Use 'what it looks like' (or 'how it looks'); 'how' + 'like' is ungrammatical in this phrase.
Countable noun 'designer' needs an article ('a designer') or plural ('designers create') in this general statement.
'Human deals' is an unnatural collocation in English; use 'human interactions', 'human activities', or 'human affairs'.
Incorrect use of possessive "people's" and missing copula 'are'; needs plural subject + be verb ('people are creative').
"Non-typical" is literal and uncommon in English; a more natural collocation is 'unconventional' or 'atypical'.
In time clauses after conjunctions like 'after', English uses the simple present (or a different phrasing: 'afterwards I will answer'), not 'will'.

annotation_items.text: AI, uh, make you more creative
annotation_items.correction: does AI make you more creative
annotation_items.explanation: Subject-verb agreement and word order for questions: with singular subject 'AI' use auxiliary 'does' + base verb 'make'.

annotation_items.text: AI couldn't suggest you a new business idea
annotation_items.correction: AI couldn't suggest a new business idea to you
annotation_items.explanation: Verb 'suggest' requires the preposition 'to' when indicating the recipient ('suggest X to you'), or use object + preposition. The original word order is incorrect.

annotation_items.correction: can suggest different ideas to you
annotation_items.explanation: Missing preposition 'to' before the recipient; 'suggest ... to you' is the natural structure here.

annotation_items.text: if you didn't try to do something by yourself before
annotation_items.correction: if you haven't tried to do something by yourself before
annotation_items.explanation: When referring to prior experience up to now, English commonly uses the present perfect ('haven't tried') rather than simple past in this conditional context.

annotation_items.text: see a lot of examples and saw a lot of examples
annotation_items.correction: have seen a lot of examples
annotation_items.explanation: Mixed tenses and repetition ('see' then 'saw')—use a consistent tense (present perfect 'have seen') or a single simple form; remove duplication.

annotation_items.text: asked the same question for me
annotation_items.explanation: Wrong preposition/structure: the person who receives the question is the indirect object ('asked me the question'), not 'for me' here.
Missing definite article before the noun phrase 'coding field' — English requires 'the' in this context ('in the coding field').

annotation_items.text: which I didn't have a chance to make before
annotation_items.correction: which I haven't had a chance to make before
annotation_items.explanation: Present perfect is preferred for experiences up to the present ('haven't had a chance').

annotation_items.text: when person can make usual things
annotation_items.correction: when a person can make usual things
annotation_items.explanation: Missing indefinite article before singular count noun 'person'.

annotation_items.correction: Or in a more unique way
annotation_items.explanation: Missing preposition 'in' (and an article) to form the phrase 'in a more ... way'.
Verb pattern error: 'suggest' is followed by a gerund or a that-clause ('suggest stopping' or 'suggest we stop'), not 'to' + infinitive.

annotation_items.text: my app's much more simpler and understandable
annotation_items.correction: my app is much simpler and more understandable
annotation_items.explanation: Double comparative ('much more simpler') is incorrect; use 'much simpler' or 'more simple'.
Wrong word form: the noun 'analysis' is required rather than the verb/participle 'analyzing'.

annotation_items.text: in spite of length of our call
annotation_items.correction: in spite of the length of our call
annotation_items.explanation: Missing definite article 'the' before 'length'.
Wrong preposition (and missing possessive). Devices normally take 'on' and require 'my' here: 'on my iPhone'.
